pattern: The cheapest way to serve a contiguous run of houses with one mailbox is always to plant it at the median, which turns the whole problem into a partition-into-contiguous-runs DP over a precomputed cost table.
hints: Sort the houses first - an optimal solution never assigns a mailbox to a non-contiguous group of houses, since swapping to keep each group contiguous never increases the total distance.
For any contiguous run of sorted houses, the mailbox position that minimizes total distance is the median of that run; precompute this cost for every (start, end) run once.
With costs precomputed, let dp[i][b] be the minimum total distance to cover the first i sorted houses using b mailboxes, transitioning by choosing where the last mailbox's run begins.

edge_inputs.description: single house needs exactly one mailbox at its own position
as many mailboxes as houses drives every distance to zero
one mailbox for a tight cluster plus a distant outlier
a single mailbox for every house, forcing the whole-array median
houses at the extreme position bounds
an even-sized run needs the correct median tie handling, not just one endpoint
unsorted input order must not change the optimal grouping

stress.description: near-max house count with a mid-range mailbox budget
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    houses = rng.sample(range(1, 10001), n)
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [houses, k]

stress.description: clustered houses in a few tight groups, testing median-cost precision
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    centers = [rng.randint(200, 9800) for _ in range(5)]
    pool = set()
    while len(pool) < n:
        c = rng.choice(centers)
        pool.add(c + rng.randint(-15, 15))
    houses = [max(1, min(10000, v)) for v in list(pool)[:n]]
    houses = list(dict.fromkeys(houses))
    k = rng.randint(1, len(houses))
    return [houses, k]
